Vercel is 𝘰𝘧𝘧𝘪𝘤𝘪𝘢𝘭𝘭𝘺 10 years old, according to the State of Delaware, the first US 🇺🇸 state 🫡. Some reflections from the journey: 1️⃣ You can just incorporate things "In America, they let you start companies easily". It's magical how fast you can get your ', 𝙸𝚗𝚌', your bank account, your LOIs, your payroll set up… Docusigns fly, investors are eager to take risk, there's an overwhelming sense of optimism around technology. Business owners are the heroes of this society. We must preserve this company-building culture at all costs. Thankful to San Francisco for being the epitome of it. 2️⃣ 10 years is the baseline It's wild to me that it feels like Vercel is just getting started. I remember prior to starting the company looking at the statistics on the median time from inception to IPO for tech companies. It was 8-10 years then and now looking like 10-14. There are many tough moments while building a company. But what's a bad day or quarter going to do if you know this is a 15-year journey? 3️⃣ It's always day zero When I registered the domain name and shipped the first company landing page, there was no Next.js. No Git integration. No CDN. No Turborepo. No AI SDK. No v0. At one point, Jamstack and static sites were all the rage. Now, agents and AI. The only way to survive in this game is to have tremendous adaptability and appetite for shipping. Our first name was "Zeit", because the only way to win is to capture the Zeitgeist. # How to build a network without "networking"
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Vercel•57K followers
6mo
A lot of younger people who’re new to the industry ask me how to do “networking”. How do you go from knowing no one in San Francisco to having a network of people that you can do business with, learn from, hire, etc. The trick is I never set out to “network”. Maybe due to Tylenol exposure in womb, or just pure intuition, but I could never fathom that I’d go to an event or reach out to someone to “network”. “Hi this is G and I’m here to network”. Instead what I focused on was creating value for others and focus on positive value exchanges. Creating open source projects is the “hyper scale” version of this. You’re networking with the world, but you’re doing so by leading with value and with content. Writing essays, teaching people things, giving lightning talks, vibe coding an experiment, all more valuable. What you’re going to notice is that networks build around value, not the other way around. Even today, whenever I reach out to someone, I try to lead with some insight, datapoint, maybe a bug report or suggestion for their product… something tangible. If they have a product or platform with APIs, I start by networking *our systems*. I’ve made amazing, close connections to people who I frequently chat with and I’ve never met IRL. I also make it a point to talk to everybody. Anons, interns, juniors, seniors, CEOs, board members, trolls. tl;DR: Focus on creating value, think long term, don’t be transactional.

Am I working with people who help me grow as a thinker? Does this project help me show that I get things done? Will this opportunity build networks in the fields I care about? At EAG London 2025, I asked a dozen professionals what made the biggest difference in landing their roles. Most worked in small EA-aligned nonprofits (5–25 people) as operations, project, or program managers. I wanted to know what skills or experience I should focus on if I’d like to follow a similar path. Here are the three answers that came up most often: 1. Networks — An obvious one, but worth highlighting. Hiring someone you’ve already seen in action feels far less risky. Tip: It’s easier to build networks if you have something concrete to ask for (e.g. feedback on a project idea). 2. Getting shit done — Straightforward, but crucial. Results speak for themselves. 3. Clear, critical thinking — This one was more novel to me. Small organisations need people who see the bigger picture and think independently about how to reach goals effectively. A few even challenged assumptions during the hiring process, not to be difficult, but to make sure the role and their skills were genuinely a good fit. With this in mind, since stepping down from EA Finland to start university last month, I’ve tried to find people and ideas to build something together from 0 to 1. 👉 Is there any 4th point you would add to the list?

Alexander Obenauer has a new series on the need for composability in operating systems. His latest is https://lnkd.in/e4aTFTK4 He's a good thinker about this stuff. And I resonate with the mission. Unlike Obenauer I suspect, I am much more wedded to text than graphics and graphic design. I love text interfaces, and one reason for that is that I just think it's much easier to do all the other good things like abstraction and composition with text than it is in a graphical environment. The Unix pipeline has just worked for decades. There are millions of ad hoc "applications" created on the fly, every day, by people piping together some Unix tools. It's so good you don't notice it. Composing GUI elements is much harder. You can kind of embed one thing in another by having one app allocate a canvas to act as a window to another. These days, it's not uncommon to use iframes in web pages to compose from multiple sources. But full integration and communication between them is hard. Admittedly partly because of necessary security concerns. But also UI elements can look different. Keyboard short-cuts can clash. And size and shape constraints from both the original app and the host app are not necessarily compatible. Web design is, when you come to think of it, a surprisingly large amount of work, just trying to fit components that come from one app, together visually. Let alone two apps. And if you hoped to nest three apps, one inside another? And if your ideal is that end users can compose multiple apps together on the fly? That's why I personally believe in text. And it's one of the reasons I was excited when the original ChatGPT appeared. Not only because of the AI. But because a chat window seems to me to be the best way to get something closer to the Unix terminal experience out to a large number of mainstream users. If I were to sit down to try to create a new UI shell for a new operating system, it would start with a chatbot as the main front-end. Internally it would deal with something like the "Intents" you have on Android. Where multiple "apps" can register to handle the same data type or task. The chatbot would route natural language requests to these apps and replies in text. Sometimes the app might need to show ad hoc GUI components (eg a drawing canvas, a colour picker) as and when needed. Behind the scenes, the bot would manage installing and routing data between the apps; storing / publishing final results etc.

Enums allow us to define a set of named values instead of relying on raw numbers — which makes designs more readable and maintainable. Here’s what I explored: .first() & .last() → Access the first and last enum elements .num() → Count the number of elements in the enum .name() → Get the name of an enum element .next() & .prev() → Traverse forward and backward through enum elements *Code:* module tb; typedef enum{red, blue, green, yellow, white, black} my_enum; my_enum vs; my_enum temp; initial begin $display("--------------------------------------------------------"); $display("value of first element of enum = %0d \n", vs.first()); $display("value of last element of enum = %0d \n", vs.last()); $display("number of element in enum = %0d \n", vs.num()); $display("name of enum = %0s \n", vs.name()); vs = green; $display("next index value of green = %0d \n", vs.next()); $display("previous index value of green = %0d \n", vs.prev()); $display("elements of enum are :"); temp = vs.first(); do begin $write("%s, ", temp.name()); temp = temp.next(); end while(temp != vs.first()); $display("\n"); temp = white; temp = temp.next(); $display("next element of white is = %0s \n", temp.name()); vs = vs.prev(); $display("previous element of green is = %0s \n", vs.name()); $display("--------------------------------------------------------"); end endmodule I even looped through all enum elements using a do-while loop, which is super handy for iterating without manually keeping track of indexes. ✅ Why this matters: Enums improve code readability, reduce bugs from magic numbers, and allow clean iteration over states or modes in your digital designs. If performance is critical to a system, performance needs to be an integral part of the design. It doesn't mean that all code should be optimized from the beginning, but it should be clear that the initial design can meet the performance criteria. Writing a huge system and then starting to measure/optimize could mean a full rewrite. Sometimes we stand stuck in front of a closed door, not noticing there’s no wall beside it. That’s where TRIZ can help IT engineers find a way forward. In the 1960s, engineers developed TRIZ - the Theory of Inventive Problem Solving. It was based on the analysis of ~40,000 patents. Over time, TRIZ has been adapted to solve non-material and abstract problems as well. Why is this relevant for IT? Because IT systems are engineering systems. Just like engines or power grids, they evolve according to certain principles and TRIZ provides a structured framework for creative problem-solving, derived from patterns in thousands of patents. It’s highly applicable to software architecture. TRIZ in Software Architecture Lets review typical scenario: monolith application refactoring without moving to microservices. TRIZ shows us there’s no wall beside it. The goal is to improve maintainability, scalability, and development speed without the distributed complexity. Here’s how TRIZ principles guide the solution: 1. Segmentation: break the monolith into a modular monolith with enforced boundaries. 2. Local Quality: allow each module to use the most appropriate technology for its specific job. 3. Preliminary Action: build a robust CI/CD pipeline and use feature toggles to make deployments safe and fast. 4. The Other Way Around: invert the database dependency. Give modules private databases and make them communicate via APIs, not direct SQL. 5. Dynamicity: implement internal circuit breakers and bulkheads so modules can handle failure gracefully without bringing down the whole system. The result? A "TRIZ-Refactored" monolith that is segmented, technologically flexible, resilient, and ready for independent development - all within a single deployable unit. TRIZ gives engineers a powerful lens to solve technical contradictions and evolve systems intelligently. It’s not just about mechanics; it’s about fundamental inventive principles. Altshuller Institute for TRIZ studies: https://www.aitriz.org/#TRIZ#SoftwareArchitecture#SoftwareEngineering#Refactoring#SystemDesign

I used AI for something nefarious this week, and I'm pretty happy with myself, actually* I was chatting to someone about their client and how a previous agency had rebuilt a site, but completely ignored all of the SEO implications of the rebuild. The situation falls to me: we need to get all 600 pages of content back so we can work through it, re-upload it, and get the page rankings back up to scratch. Of course, we can't just download 600 pages by going directly into the server, so we need a more novel approach. My first question to Gemini was, "Can you scrape this website?" to which it replied no; it could scrape a single page for me, but it wouldn't be able to scrape the whole site. I then did a Google search and found some tools, but they would download the full code of the page rather than just the clean content, which would cause a headache in the long run. Back to Gemini I go—and ask it to build a scraping tool for me. Sure, I could learn how to do it and go back into the world of Python, but this is what I would call a very specific, short-lived project with no need for maintainability or security. It just needed to be a fire-and-forget piece of script that would crawl a whole website and drop the content into a CSV file for me. 30 seconds later—I've got the script. I run it (and of course it fails), but I can see the problems and after a quick tête-à-tête with Gemini, we got the script finished. I spent some time in the network logs of OpenAI's new Atlas browser, and there’s a fundamental technical shift here that marketers need to understand: First, the basics: Atlas is a browser where the ChatGPT is the default interface. It's built to answer questions and complete tasks directly, without necessarily sending you to a URL. Now the strategy: most marketers' first reaction will be that this just means 'fewer clicks.' I think that misses the real story, which you can only see in the logs. The difference isn't just what Atlas returns; it's how it gets the answer. Classic ChatGPT works like a souped-up search engine. It runs a series queries, scrapes the snippets, and summarizes them. It's entirely dependent on what a search crawler has already indexed. Atlas is a fundamentally different machine. It's an agent and it can see and interact with everything (even JS). It's executing DOM clicks on size selectors, dismissing modals, and scrolling through dynamic product grid. It's interacting with the live, JavaScript-rendered front end. But I think this isn't just about Atlas. This is a clear preview of where Google AI Mode is headed, and a peek into the future of the SERP: led by chat with blue links as secondary information. The user adoption of Atlas itself doesn't matter bc this is the beginning of chat-first browsing and 'agent experience' as an entire pillar of the web. Winning in this new environment will come down to agent readiness: Think about how Atlas resolves a query like "waterproof trail shoes under $150 delivered by Friday." It isn't sitting around matching keywords, it's executing a task against your live site. If your JavaScript is slow, or your checkout flow is clunky, the agent will fail. Let's be honest: most brands' front ends are a mess of JavaScript and clunky user flows, many sites will inadvertently block Atlas' bot, and their design is optimized for humans who know how to get around the web, not for an impatient agent. That's the entire opportunity. The advantage goes to the companies who do the unglamorous work of making their sites fast, clean, and scriptable. These are the strategies I'll be watching: - Optimizing for agent usability, not just crawler indexability. This is the moment when a fast, scriptable, client-side rendered front end becomes a major competitive advantage. - Treating your website like an API for agents. Every interactive element is a chokepoint that must be fast and reliable. - Doubling down on structured data. Structured data will be the ground truth an agent can use to validate the information it finds on your live site. At its core, this is about shifting from optimizing for discovery to optimizing for action. The game is no longer just about being found; it's about being usable by an AI. Atlas is live @ chatgpt.com/atlas Spend an hour looking at how it actually interacts with sites in your vertical. Happy optimizing 👀

Quick Tip for Jetpack Compose Developers 💡 Stop passing ViewModel instances directly into your composable functions. 🚫 The Anti-Pattern: fun MyComposable(viewModel: MyViewModel) ✅ The Best Practice (State Hoisting): fun MyComposable(uiState: MyUiState, onDoSomething: () -> Unit) Why? As the official documentation warns, ViewModels are not part of the Composition. Passing them down can tightly couple your UI to the data source and lead to potential memory leaks, as the composable might hold a reference it shouldn't. By passing only the state data and lambdas for events, you create composables that are: Easier to test in isolation. Highly reusable. Less prone to lifecycle-related bugs. Do you follow this pattern strictly in your code?#JetpackCompose#AndroidDev#Kotlin#MobileDevelopment#SoftwareArchitecture#Android

We’re actively collaborating with them, bringing the perspective of some of the world’s largest enterprises to the table to ensure these new standards reflect their needs. That’s the idea behind our merchant-first foundation for agentic commerce: an open framework that keeps merchants in command of payments, data, and risk, no matter which protocol or platform the shopper’s agent uses. Yesterday we introduced the AI SDK 3.0 with Generative UI support. In 2023, Casetext was acquired for $650M because they "removed" hallucinations from their platform, through eval-driven development. When LLMs came out, Casetext had already existed for 10 years. But they quickly pivoted into GenAI and found a solid use case in legal analysis. The use case worked because lawyers and paralegals are drowning in work and documents. (One of the reasons so much money is being poured into legal-tech currently). However, they also don't want their license and practice to be in jeopardy if an AI they use hallucinates facts and citations. What Casetext figured out so early on, and what is only now gaining traction, is something called eval-driven development. The paradigm itself is not new, it's in fact similar to test-driven development in software engineering. Here's what you do 1/ Break down a multi-step workflow into a series of micro steps. 2/ Build lots and lots of evals (i.e. input prompt & output pairs) for each micro step. 3/ Improve prompts and models until all evals for each micro step pass. As a result, your rate of hallucinations goes down considerably. And that is what justified the sky high valuation of Casetext.

⛈︎ The call is coming from inside the cloud The (D)DoS attack landscape of the internet has changed dramatically over the past few years. A lot of attacks on Vercel customers are increasingly coming from within the hyperscalers. Just yesterday we blocked a targeted distributed attack peaking at 1,000s+ of concurrent IPs. We suspect at least 17,949 machines were compromised. A few interesting takeaways: • The clouds are not keeping up. This specific attack pattern has been recurring and the resources they control are significant. • You can't bank on IP reputation signals. These IPs are very high reputation. Vetted customers have been compromised and their machines are being mis-used. We've had to develop very surgical mitigations to prevent impacting legitimate traffic coming from the same ASNs. • Security is the core advantage of managed services. The most likely culprit here is boxes running outdated software in a VM or VPS with manually configured SSH access and networking getting breached. From the provider's perspective, these machines are a black box. It's hard to understand if they're secure, it's hard to help the customer, and it's hard to understand if their traffic patterns are malicious. tl;DR: you can sleep soundly at night that Vercel is both providing you a more secure compute primitive, while also protecting you from these nasty attacks out of the box
Hearing this makes me wonder what more cloud providers could do to identify malicious usage of resources and stop it at the source. One of the first applications I deployed to the cloud after my bootcamp was hit by a DDoS attack and taken down. I shortly redeployed, adding rate-limiters and didn't have any further issues. It's scary to think about how much attackers have evolved since then and how they are leveraging the scalability of the cloud to do harm. More recently, I've been deploying a lot of applications to Vercel, and I've never faced similar issues, despite these advances in attack patterns. Keep up the great work!
I think AI can play a key role in cybersecurity by identifying malicious patterns faster than humans, reducing the effectiveness of DDoS attacks and minimizing potential downtime

The internet was abuzz last week with McMaster-Carr's website being such a great example of performance. Which it is: check it out for yourself at[mcmaster.com](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http://mcmaster.com&urlhash=8SoW&trk=public_post-text)So, our community set out to recreate it using[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text). In just a few hours, NextMaster was born. With the help of AI and[https://v0.dev](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://v0.dev&urlhash=uj6f&trk=public_post-text),[Ethan Niser](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ethanniser?trk=public_post-text)had a prototype up and running. And then over the weekend,[Rhys Sullivan](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rhyssullivan?trk=public_post-text)and Arman Kumaraswamy perfected it.
NextMaster is open source and a great learning resource on the latest and greatest[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text)capabilities:
•Partial Prerendering, which makes all loads snappy
•Server-side Streaming, which helps personalize the page without client side JS and performance penalties. This is used for logged-in states and carts.
•Prefetching, which makes all the client side loads really snappy. •Image optimization
What's more, we set out to demonstrate the scale of[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text). McMaster-Carr features an impressive 700,000+ product catalog. We used[OpenAI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/openai?trk=public_post-text)'s batch inference capabilities to generate 1,000,000 imagined products, complete with titles and descriptions.
In short, this app \*feels\* like a static or CDN-cached website, but it's in reality a fully dynamic, server-rendered experience. While CDNs got us here, the future is in hybrid experiences that don't de-opt to expensive and slow client-side JS rendering.
This is a blueprint, I believe, for the future of e-commerce on the web. I just want to highlight this uses the `onmousedown`. If only that had been the default behavior of onclick, humans collectively would've saved a nearly inconceivable amount of time.
Consider there's 8 billion humans on the planet, and each had clicked a <button onclick=...> once today. They lose say 100 milliseconds each time without onmousedown. Well that's 800 million seconds. Which is 13 million minutes, which is 500 thousand hours, which is 9,259 days, which 25 years of human time lost!
This started as a dumb thought, but I don't think we often consider how much time humans lose waiting for computers. I can't comment on this specific implementation without more[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post_comment-text)experience, but I do appreciate how much concern Vercel puts into performance. While the popularity of web-first approaches have inevitably brought challenges and growing pains, it usually feels as though software performance has not scaled with hardware at all. I suppose it's partly because "There are two ways of constructing a software design: One way is to make it so simple that there are obviously no deficiencies, and the other way is to make it so complicated that there are no obvious deficiencies." The former is performant yet difficult to arrive at.

The internet was abuzz last week with McMaster-Carr's website being such a great example of performance. Which it is: check it out for yourself at[mcmaster.com](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http://mcmaster.com&urlhash=8SoW&trk=public_post_reshare-text)So, our community set out to recreate it using[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post_reshare-text). In just a few hours, NextMaster was born. With the help of AI and[https://v0.dev](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://v0.dev&urlhash=uj6f&trk=public_post_reshare-text),[Ethan Niser](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ethanniser?trk=public_post_reshare-text)had a prototype up and running. And then over the weekend,[Rhys Sullivan](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rhyssullivan?trk=public_post_reshare-text)and Arman Kumaraswamy perfected it.
NextMaster is open source and a great learning resource on the latest and greatest[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post_reshare-text)capabilities:
•Partial Prerendering, which makes all loads snappy
•Server-side Streaming, which helps personalize the page without client side JS and performance penalties. This is used for logged-in states and carts.
•Prefetching, which makes all the client side loads really snappy. •Image optimization
What's more, we set out to demonstrate the scale of[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post_reshare-text). McMaster-Carr features an impressive 700,000+ product catalog. We used[OpenAI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/openai?trk=public_post_reshare-text)'s batch inference capabilities to generate 1,000,000 imagined products, complete with titles and descriptions.
In short, this app \*feels\* like a static or CDN-cached website, but it's in reality a fully dynamic, server-rendered experience. While CDNs got us here, the future is in hybrid experiences that don't de-opt to expensive and slow client-side JS rendering.
This is a blueprint, I believe, for the future of e-commerce on the web. You can deploy your own to[Vercel](https://www.linkedin.com/company/vercel?trk=public_post_reshare-text), or head here and just click around to experience the speed:[https://lnkd.in/g3hXg-gx](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/g3hXg-gx&urlhash=-5ts&trk=public_post_reshare-text)

I'm having a hard time wrapping my head around how it's possible to have a dynamic site be this ridiculously performant. Pre-rendered, static sites used to be the only way, and they still somehow weren't this fast.
Browsing this site feels like it anticipates your next move (like a magical prerender hint) and the next page seemingly loads before your onmouseup completes (semi-nerd moment there). So hundreds upon hundreds of requests, but when you're optimized for caching and running on edge computing, who cares?
Either way, extremely impressive work. Can't wait to see this on my favorite, real sites!

News Alert: OpenAI Switches from Next.JS to Remix!
OpenAI has recently made a notable shift in their technology stack, moving from Next.JS to Remix for their web projects. While Next.JS remains a powerful and widely used framework, OpenAI’s transition highlights a strategic preference for Remix’s advanced capabilities in web performance and routing.
Here’s why OpenAI switched to Remix:
1. Improved Server-Side Rendering (SSR): Remix’s SSR enables more efficient, dynamic data loading, ideal for real-time applications.
2. Flexible Routing: Remix excels in handling nested routes, simplifying the development and maintenance of complex applications.
3. Performance Optimization: With built-in prefetching, Remix ensures faster load times and a smoother user experience.
4. Unified Data Management: Remix simplifies data handling by integrating server and client-side management, reducing complexity and boosting performance.
Note: Next.JS remains a robust, versatile framework widely favored for static and server-side rendered applications. Both Remix and Next.JS have unique strengths, and the choice depends on the specific needs of each project.

It's interesting to know why OpenAI moved from[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text)to Remix.
OpenAI recently shifted from[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text)to Remix, driven by Remix’s performance, enhanced developer experience, and specific capabilities that align with OpenAI's needs:
𝟭) 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗲𝗿-𝗖𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗰 𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗴𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗘𝗻𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁: Remix focuses on server-side rendering (SSR) and progressive enhancement, making it easier to build fast, SEO-friendly applications that prioritise server logic. This aligns with OpenAI’s focus on performance and efficient server-side management.
𝟮) 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗺𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗱𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗟𝗼𝗮𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴: Remix offers powerful data-loading patterns that handle nested routes and layouts efficiently. It optimises data fetching, making apps faster and more responsive by reducing client-side complexity.
𝟯) 𝗣𝗲𝗿𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗢𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗶𝘇𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀: Remix eliminates unnecessary JavaScript, optimises assets, and leverages modern web standards. This approach reduces page load times and improves user experience, which is crucial for data-heavy platforms like OpenAI.
𝟰) 𝗙𝗼𝗰𝘂𝘀𝗼𝗻𝗪𝗲𝗯𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗿𝗱𝘀: Remix prioritises standard web APIs, creating accessible, resilient, and performant web apps. This approach aligns well with the needs of modern web applications.
𝟱) 𝗘𝗻𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗱𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲: Remix’s features like route-based code splitting, improved error handling, and intuitive nested routing provide a more productive workflow compared to[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text).
𝟲) 𝗙𝗹𝗲𝘅𝗶𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗥𝗼𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗡𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗱𝗟𝗮𝘆𝗼𝘂𝘁𝘀: Remix’s routing system allows for intuitive and efficient layout and route definition, ideal for complex applications with nested UI structures.
𝟳) 𝗥𝗼𝗯𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗘𝗱𝗴𝗲𝗖𝗮𝘀𝗲𝗛𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴: Remix offers better fallback mechanisms for network issues and errors, enhancing overall app resilience and user experience.
